subject: Full Stack Development
text:
What will the following code output?

console.log(typeof null);
explanation: This is a well-known JavaScript quirk. 'typeof null' returns 'object' due to a bug in the original JavaScript implementation that was never fixed for backward compatibility.

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: JavaScript evaluates left to right. First line: 1+'2' becomes '12' (string concat), then '12'+3 becomes '123'. Second line: 1+2 becomes 3 (number), then 3+'3' becomes '33'.

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Because 'var' is function-scoped (not block-scoped), all three setTimeout callbacks share the same 'i', which is 3 by the time they execute after the loop completes.

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Unlike 'var', 'let' is block-scoped, so each iteration of the loop creates a new binding of 'i'. Each setTimeout callback closes over its own 'i' value.

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Arrays and objects are converted to strings during '+' concatenation. []+[] becomes ''+'' = ''. []+{} becomes ''+'[object Object]' = '[object Object]'. {}+[] in a console.log context is treated as a value, becoming '[object Object]'+'' = '[object Object]'.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: Which method is used to convert a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
explanation: JSON.parse() converts a JSON-formatted string into a JavaScript object. JSON.stringify() does the reverse operation.

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: This demonstrates a closure. The inner function retains access to 'count' from the outer function's scope even after outer() has returned, and the value persists across calls.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the difference between '==' and '===' in JavaScript?
explanation: '==' performs type coercion before comparing values, while '===' compares both value and type without any coercion (strict equality).

options.text: '==' compares values with type coercion, '===' compares value and type strictly
'==' is faster but does the same comparison as '==='
'===' is used only for strings, '==' for numbers
There is no functional difference between them

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: foo() runs synchronously until the first 'await'. So: console.log('C') runs -> foo() is called -> 'A' logs synchronously -> await pauses foo, control returns to caller -> 'D' logs -> microtask queue runs -> 'B' logs.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What does Array.prototype.map() return?
explanation: map() creates and returns a brand new array by applying the given function to every element of the original array, without modifying the original array.

options.text: A new array with transformed elements
The original array, modified in place

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: filter(n => n > 1) keeps elements greater than 1, giving [2, 3]. Then map(n => n * 2) doubles each, giving [4, 6].

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the difference between Object.freeze() and const for an object?
explanation: 'const' only prevents reassignment of the variable itself, but the object's properties can still be modified. Object.freeze() makes the object's properties immutable, preventing any changes to existing properties.

options.text: const prevents reassignment of the variable; Object.freeze() prevents modification of the object's properties
They do exactly the same thing
Object.freeze() prevents reassignment; const prevents property modification
Neither prevents any kind of mutation

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Destructuring extracts 'a' as 1, and the rest operator '...rest' collects all remaining properties into a new object, giving { b: 2 }.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is JSX in React?
explanation: JSX is a syntax extension for JavaScript that allows writing HTML-like syntax directly within JavaScript code, which gets compiled into React.createElement() calls.

options.text: A syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like code in JavaScript
A separate templating language unrelated to JavaScript

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Every time setCount is called with a new value, React re-renders the component, so 'Render' logs to console on every click, along with the updated count being displayed.

options.text: 'Render' logs on initial mount and again on every button click
'Render' logs only once, ever, no matter how many times the button is clicked
The component never re-renders since useState caches the value
An error occurs because count is not initialized properly

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the purpose of the dependency array in useEffect?
explanation: The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs. If a value in the array changes between renders, the effect runs again. An empty array means the effect runs only once after the initial render.

options.text: It controls when the effect re-runs based on which values have changed
It lists the components that depend on this effect
It defines CSS dependencies for styling
It has no functional purpose, it's purely documentation

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: This has a stale closure bug: the interval callback always references the 'seconds' value from the initial render (0), so it keeps setting seconds to 1 repeatedly instead of incrementing. It also lacks cleanup (clearInterval), causing a memory leak on unmount. The fix is to use the functional update form: setSeconds(s => s + 1), and return a cleanup function.

options.text: It has a stale closure bug and missing interval cleanup
useState cannot be used with numbers
There is no issue, the code works correctly

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What does useMemo primarily help with?
explanation: useMemo memoizes the result of an expensive computation, recalculating it only when its dependencies change, which helps avoid unnecessary recalculations on every render.

options.text: Memoizing expensive computed values between renders
Replacing the need for useState entirely

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the correct way to update state based on the previous state in React?
explanation: Using the functional form, setCount(prev => prev + 1), guarantees you're working with the most current state value, especially important when multiple updates happen in quick succession (e.g. in a loop or async callback).

subject: Full Stack Development
text: In a functional component, which hook is the equivalent of componentDidMount from class components?
explanation: useEffect with an empty dependency array ([]) runs only once after the initial render, mimicking the behavior of componentDidMount.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: Can a child component directly modify a prop passed to it from a parent?
explanation: Props are read-only in React. A child component should never directly mutate a prop; instead, it should call a function passed down from the parent (often via props) to request a state change.

options.text: No, props are read-only and should never be mutated directly
Yes, props can be reassigned freely inside the child
Only numeric props can be modified
Only if the prop is an object

subject: Full Stack Development
text: Why is using the array index as a 'key' prop in a list considered an anti-pattern when the list can be reordered?
explanation: When list items are reordered, added, or removed, using the index as a key can cause React to misidentify which DOM elements correspond to which data, leading to incorrect rendering, lost component state, or unnecessary re-renders.

options.text: It can cause React to misidentify items during reordering, leading to rendering bugs
React does not allow numeric keys at all
There is no actual issue with using index as key

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is Node.js primarily used for?
explanation: Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine that allows JavaScript to be executed outside the browser, commonly used to build server-side applications and APIs.

options.text: Running JavaScript outside the browser, typically for server-side applications

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: Synchronous code runs first: '1' and '4' log immediately. Then microtasks (Promises) run before macrotasks (setTimeout), so '3' logs next, followed by '2'.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the difference between 'require' (CommonJS) and 'import' (ES Modules) in Node.js?
explanation: require() is synchronous and loads modules at runtime (CommonJS, traditional Node.js style), while import is part of ES Modules, supports static analysis, and can be asynchronous; Node.js needs explicit configuration (e.g. "type": "module" in package.json) to use ES Modules natively.

options.text: require is CommonJS and synchronous; import is ES Modules and supports static analysis
They are completely identical with no differences
import only works in the browser, never in Node.js

subject: Full Stack Development
text: Which HTTP method is conventionally used to update an existing resource entirely (replacing it)?
explanation: PUT is conventionally used to replace an entire resource. PATCH is used for partial updates, POST for creating new resources, and DELETE for removing resources.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the key difference between PUT and PATCH HTTP methods?
explanation: PUT typically replaces the entire resource with the provided payload, while PATCH applies a partial update, modifying only the specified fields.

options.text: PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH applies a partial update
PUT is for reading data; PATCH is for writing data
They are functionally identical in every REST API
PATCH is used only for deleting resources

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What HTTP status code should be returned when a resource is successfully created via a POST request?
explanation: 201 Created is the standard status code indicating that a new resource was successfully created as a result of the request, typically along with a Location header pointing to the new resource.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What does HTTP status code 404 indicate?
explanation: 404 Not Found indicates that the server could not find the requested resource. The client may have requested a URL that doesn't exist or has been removed.

options.text: The requested resource was not found
The server encountered an internal error

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the difference between HTTP status codes 401 and 403?
explanation: 401 Unauthorized means the client is not authenticated (no valid credentials provided), while 403 Forbidden means the client is authenticated but does not have permission to access the resource.

options.text: 401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not authorized
401 and 403 mean exactly the same thing
401 is for servers; 403 is for clients

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the main advantage of using JWT (JSON Web Tokens) over traditional session-based authentication?
explanation: JWTs are stateless, meaning the server doesn't need to store session data — all necessary information is encoded in the token itself, which makes JWTs well-suited for distributed systems and horizontal scaling.

options.text: JWTs are stateless, removing the need for server-side session storage
JWTs are always more secure than any other method
JWTs eliminate the need for HTTPS

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What are the three parts of a JWT, separated by dots?
explanation: A JWT consists of three Base64Url-encoded parts: the Header (algorithm and token type), the Payload (claims/data), and the Signature (used to verify the token hasn't been tampered with).

subject: Full Stack Development
text: Which CSS property is used to control the spacing between flex items in a flex container?
explanation: The 'gap' property controls spacing between flex or grid items directly within the container, without needing margins on individual items.

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the difference between 'position: relative' and 'position: absolute' in CSS?
explanation: 'relative' positions an element relative to its own normal position, while 'absolute' positions an element relative to its nearest positioned (non-static) ancestor, removing it from the normal document flow.

options.text: relative positions based on the element's normal position; absolute positions relative to the nearest positioned ancestor
They behave identically in all cases
absolute is always relative to the viewport only
relative removes the element from the document flow

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the main purpose of an ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) tool like Hibernate or Sequelize?
explanation: An ORM allows developers to interact with a relational database using object-oriented code instead of writing raw SQL queries, mapping database tables to classes/objects automatically.

options.text: To map database tables to objects, allowing database interaction via object-oriented code
To replace the need for a database entirely
To handle only frontend state management

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the N+1 query problem commonly encountered when using an ORM?
explanation: The N+1 problem occurs when fetching a list of N parent records triggers 1 query for the list, followed by N additional queries (one per record) to fetch related data, instead of using a single optimized join query — causing significant performance issues at scale.

options.text: Fetching N records triggers 1 query for the list plus N additional queries for related data, instead of one efficient join
A bug that causes exactly one extra row to be inserted into a table
An error that occurs when a primary key is duplicated
A limitation where ORMs can only handle N+1 tables in a join

subject: Full Stack Development
explanation: run() is called, executes synchronously until 'await delay()', which pauses execution and returns control to the caller. 'started' logs immediately. After ~100ms, the Promise resolves and 'done' logs.

options.text: started and done log at the exact same time

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What happens if a Promise rejection is not caught with .catch() or a try/catch block in an async function?
explanation: An unhandled Promise rejection results in an 'UnhandledPromiseRejection' warning/error, and in Node.js this can crash the process in newer versions, depending on configuration. It will not be silently ignored.

options.text: It results in an unhandled promise rejection warning/error
The rejection is silently ignored with no consequence

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What problem does the Context API in React primarily solve?
explanation: Context API solves 'prop drilling' — the need to pass props through many intermediate components that don't need the data themselves, just to get it to a deeply nested child.

options.text: Prop drilling — passing props through many intermediate components unnecessarily
Connecting to a backend database directly

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What does CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) primarily control?
explanation: CORS is a browser security mechanism that controls whether a web page from one origin (domain) is allowed to make requests to a server on a different origin, preventing unauthorized cross-origin requests by default.

options.text: Whether a web page can make requests to a server on a different origin
How fast a server responds to requests
The encryption algorithm used for HTTPS
How CSS styles are shared between pages

subject: Full Stack Development
text: What is the primary difference between XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) and CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks?
explanation: XSS involves injecting malicious scripts into a trusted website that then execute in users' browsers (exploiting trust the user has in the site). CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into making unwanted requests to a site where they're already logged in (exploiting trust the site has in the user).

options.text: XSS injects malicious scripts into a site; CSRF tricks an authenticated user into making unwanted requests
They are exactly the same attack with different names
XSS only affects servers; CSRF only affects databases
CSRF requires no authentication at all to function
